text:
Kaedwen is a realm thrust between the Dragon Mountains to the north, the Blue Mountains to the east, and impassable forests to the west. There rule the hereditary kings of the House of Topp. Their first capital was ancient Ban Ard, but in the year 1130 post Resurectionem King Dagread granted that stronghold to the wizards for a school, and removed the capital to Ard Carraigh, which lies in the middle of the realm.

Other chief towns of Kaedwen are Ban Fearg, Daevon, Ban Fillim and Ban Gleann.

The emblem of the Topps and of the whole realm has since time out of mind been the unicorn - d'or, licorne effrayée de sable.

Around the royal lands of Kaedwen lie the common border lands, called marches, or marchias. These are entrusted to the governance of margraves, that is to say counts, hereditary or appointed by the king. The name comes from this, that those lands are as a forward guard and, by pushing ever farther onward, are to carry conquest forth and wrest new lands for Kaedwen from the elves, shift the borders, and set ever farther their marks, id est marches. These marches are: the Western, the Upper, the Lakeland and the Lower.

Baldwin Adovardo, Regni Caedvenie Nova Descriptio








Chapter One


Geralt, despite the best will in the world - and for rather weighty reasons - simply could not focus on the headman's prattle. All his attention was consumed by the great stuffed crow on the headman's table. The crow, eying the witcher with its glass eye, stood on a base of clay painted green, both the crow's legs sunk into that clay. So the crow, for all its absolutely lifelike appearance, could in no way be alive; of that there was no question. Why then, Geralt could not stop wondering, had the crow already blinked that glass eye at him several times? Could it be magic? Hardly, for his witcher's medallion had not twitched or vibrated, not once, not in the least. Was it a hallucination, then? A delusion? Brought on, perhaps, by being struck on the head a few times?

"I'll repeat the question," Headman Bulava repeated the question. "I'll repeat it, though I am not one for repeating myself."

Headman Bulava had already assured Geralt several times that he was not one for repeating himself. Even so, he kept repeating himself at every turn. Plainly he liked it, though he was not one for it.

"I'll repeat my question: what was it really about? What did you have against that deserter, that you hacked him up so horribly? Some old grievance? Because I simply don't believe, you see, that it was about that peasant and his daughter's maiden honour. That you supposedly rushed to the rescue. Like some fucking knight-errant."




The crow eyed him. Geralt moved his hands, bound behind his back, trying to get the blood flowing. The rope was biting painfully into his wrists. Behind him he could hear the heavy breathing of a village bruiser. The bruiser stood right behind him, and Geralt was sure he was only waiting for a pretext to smash him in the ear again with his fist.

Headman Bulava snorted, sprawled in his chair, and thrust out his belly and his velvet doublet. Geralt stared at the front of the doublet and could make out what the headman had eaten today, yesterday and the day before. And that at least one of those dishes had been in tomato sauce.

"I had thought," said the headman at last, "that I would never have to set eyes on any of you witchers. No witcher has been seen for years. They were saying that after the year eleven ninety-four hardly anyone came through alive, up there in the mountains. Then word went round that the rest had died out as well, of hunger or plague. And there you have it, one of your sort shows up right in my village. And the first thing he does is commit murder. Then, caught red-handed, he has the gall to invoke some fucking edicts."

"By virtue of the personal edict of the year eleven fifty," Geralt rasped, clearing his throat, "issued by Dagread, King of Kaedwen and the Border Marches, primo: witchers are permitted to practise their profession freely within the territories of the Kingdom and the Marches, and are exempted from the jurisdiction of local authorities..."

"First of all primo," Bulava cut in sharply, "it will soon be half a century since Dagread turned to dust, and his personal autocratic edicts with him. Second of all primo, no king is going to exempt anything here from anything, because the king is in Ard Carraigh, far from here, and here local authority rules. Meaning me. And third of all primo, brother, you were not put under arrest for practising your trade but for homicide. Catching werewolves and killing leshens, that's witcher business. But as for butchering people, no king ever granted you that privilege."

"I acted in defence..."

"Daryl!"

The bruiser obediently slammed his fist into Geralt, this time in the back of the neck.

"Your repeating yourself" - the headman looked up at the ceiling - "is irritating. Do you know what irritation can lead to? Even in so calm a man as me?"

The crow peered with its glass eye. Geralt said nothing.

"You," Bulava said at last, "are no witcher. You are a defect. You require repair. Best send you back to that mountain Siedliszcze of yours people talk about. I don't know how things are done there among your lot. Maybe a botch-up like you gets taken apart for parts, to be used to make new, better witchers. Because that's how it's done up there with you, isn't it? Witchers are put together out of different human bits, stitched or glued, or however it goes. People say all sorts. So then, enough idle jawing... I'll dispatch you, failed witcher, back to the mountains, beyond the Gwenllech. In a week."

Geralt said nothing.

"You won't even ask why in a week?" The headman bared his yellow teeth. "You do like invoking edicts and laws. Well then, I'm for the law too. And the law says strangers are not allowed to carry weapons here in the district. And you came in here armed."

Geralt wanted to object that he had not come in, but had been dragged in. But he did not get the chance.

"Punishment," Bulava pronounced, "is twenty strokes of the lash. The sentence will be carried out by Daryl here present, and he has a heavy hand. You won't be on your feet again for a week. Come on, take him. Out to the square with him, tie him to the post...

"Hold, hold," said a man entering the room in a drab cloak, heavily soiled about the hem, stopping the bruisers. "What's got you, Bulava, in such a hurry for the post and the whip? You want the witcher crippled? No indeed, none of that. I need the witcher whole and healthy at the works.

"And what are you doing here," the headman said, planting his fists on his hips, "meddling in my punishments, Blaufall? It's enough I have to put up with you forever dragging people out of the village for corvee. But don't meddle in my jurisdiction, that's not your concern, this is my jurisdiction. A crime must be punished...

"Hell, not a crime," Blaufall cut him off. "There's no offense here of any kind, there's self-defence and saving people. Don't make faces, don't make faces, I've got a witness here. Step up, farmer. Come on, don't be afraid. Speak. What and how.




Geralt recognized the peasant. The very same one he had saved from being robbed yesterday, and who had bolted for the forest instead of thanking him. The father of the girl he remembered stripped down to her shift.

"I bear witness..." the peasant forced out, pointing a finger at Geralt. "I give my word that this selfsame young man hurried to save me from the robbers... Saved my property... Rescued my daughter from defilement... Delivered the innocent maid from robbers' claws...

"And that deserter," Blaufall prompted, "went at him with an axe, and the young man was only defending himself. Self-defence! Confirm it, farmer, that was how it was.

"That was how it was... Just so! Master Headman, that young man is not guilty!" The peasant was pale and unnaturally loud. "Master Headman! Set him free, I beg you. And here... Please, take this... By way of, hmm... Perhaps there were expenses, or losses... I'd be glad to make them good...

Bowing obsequiously, the peasant handed the headman a little purse. Bulava slipped it into the pocket of his billowy breeches so quickly and deftly that the purse did not so much as jingle.

"Self-defence!" he snorted. "He hacked a man apart with a sword. Innocent young man... I'd have him..."

They went out into the square. The bruisers shoved Geralt ahead of them without untying his hands.

"And you, Blaufall," the headman asked, "what's got you in such a tearing hurry that you even dragged a witness in here for me? You need this witcher that badly?




"What, you don't know? We're laying a road, the Great Road. It's to run from Ard Carraigh through the forests all the way to Hengfors. And not just any old track either, not some path, but a high road, dry and level, laid with logs and fascines so wagons and teams can pass. It's important business, this Great Road. There'll be heavy trade along it, between our lands, that is, and the North. They say the king himself ordered that it be hurried along. And there are monsters in the forests and the marshes there, and every so often some laborer dies, killed by a monster or carried off..."

“And since when do you care about workmen? You were always saying a workman didn’t matter; if one was gone, there’d be another...”

“Fuck the workmen, most of them are corvée hands. But every so often a monster does in one of my foremen, and that throws the whole job into disarray, the whole work front goes to hell. Ah, what’s the use of talking. I’m telling you, I need the witcher or I’ll miss the deadline, and it’s not just that the bonus will go to the devil, they might even send an inspection. And an inspection...”

“Always finds something,” Bulava nodded with understanding. “Either materials sold off on the side, or padded estimates, or else...”

“Don’t stray off the subject.” Blaufall grimaced. “Let the witcher out at once, no delay. I’m taking him straight to the works... Oh, for fuck’s sake... What’s going on?”

“The old hands from the garrison.” The headman shaded his eyes with his hand. “Master Carleton’s soldiers.”



Into the village square, kicking up dust and scattering chickens, a dozen or so horsemen came galloping in. Armed men. Dressed colourfully, brightly, and rather raggedly. Only the two at their head were better turned out. The commander, a moustached man in a moosehide jerkin, with a gilded pendant and a hat with a plume of ostrich feathers. And a long-haired elf with a band around his brow, in the green uniform of a scout.

“Captain Reisz Carleton,” Bulava greeted him, coming forward to meet him. “Welcome, welcome. To what do I owe the honour?”

Captain Reisz Carleton bent from the saddle and spat hard. Then he signalled to the scout. The elf rode up to the post with the crossbeam and deftly flipped a noosed rope over the timber.

“Oh-ho.” Bulava planted his hands on his hips and checked whether the bruisers were standing behind him. “Looks like the captain’s come to my village for a hanging. Ah, and I can even see whose lot it is to swing today. I can see, I can see, those two there in bonds... Ha, so the captain’s caught deserters from his garrison! The very same ones who were out in the woods here attacking peasants and girls?”

“Them,” Captain Reisz Carleton twirled his moustache, “I’ve no intention of hanging. They’ll run the gauntlet, both of them, down the lane of rods. So they remember it. And that’s all. I’ve too few men to hang them for every trifle. And too few to let some stray kill them for me with impunity.”

The captain straightened in the saddle and raised his voice, speaking not only to the headman but also to the village bruisers, Blaufall, his lackeys, and the little crowd of villagers already gathering.



“What am I supposed to punish my soldiers for? For what? For going absent without leave? For wanting to fuck a girl? Why, we’re out here at the garrison like it’s the end of the world, like exile, like a punishment posting. There’s no beer here, no women either... Why be surprised if the lads sometimes slip off on the sly and grab themselves some...”

"What the hell for," Reisz Carleton raised his voice, "are wenches wandering through the woods? And this farmer here, what, he had to take that girl of his by that very way? Couldn't he have left her at home? So why be surprised the lads wanted to... I do not approve of it! I do not approve, but I understand! Master Aelvarr? Is it ready there?"

"Ready, Captain."

"Then bring that witcher here, Bulava. He killed a soldier of mine, he'll hang. A fearsome example must be set. And don't cut him down, Headman, let him hang there, as a warning to the others."

Blaufall stepped forward, gave every sign of wanting to speak, then desisted. The bruisers seized Geralt, but they stood there uncertainly. As it turned out, rightly so.

All at once it grew strangely quiet. And as though something cold had blown through.

Into the village square, from behind the barns, there came, ever so slowly, a black horse, black as soot. It bore a rider. White-haired, in a black leather jerkin studded at the shoulders with silver rivets. Above the rider's right shoulder jutted the hilts of two swords.

Ever so slowly, with grace even, the black horse passed the villagers and the headman. To halt before Captain Carleton's cavalry.

For a moment there was silence. Then the black horse tossed its head. The rings of the bit jingled.

"Headman Bulava," said the white-haired rider into the silence, "will release the young witcher at once. He will return his horse, weapons, and belongings. At once."

"Yes..." the headman coughed. "Yes indeed, Master Holt. At once..."

"Captain Carleton." The rider inclined his head slightly. "Greetings."

"Witcher Preston Holt." Reisz Carleton touched the brim of his hat. "Greetings."

"Captain," the rider raised his voice, "be so good as to remove from here that elf, his rope, and the rest of your underlings. You are no longer needed here. Today's lynching is called off."

"Indeed?" The captain drew himself up in the saddle and rested a hand on his sword guard. "Are you that sure of yourself, witcher?"

"Yes, that sure. Farewell. Headman, is the lad released? His things returned?"

"You son of a bitch!" one of Carleton's horsemen screamed, wrenching his sword from its scabbard and jerking his mount forward. "I'll..."

He did not finish. The rider called Preston Holt raised his hand and made a brief gesture. The air howled and whistled, the villagers clapped their hands over their ears. The horseman screamed, flew out of the saddle like a stone from a sling, and crashed down heavily and limp straight under his comrades' hooves. Their horses shied, snorted, stamped, tossed their heads, one reared. The fallen man's horse bolted off between the cottages, kicking and flinging its hindquarters.

It grew very quiet.

"Anyone else?" Preston Holt raised his gloved hand. "Anyone willing to stand up? Play the hero? No? I thought not. Farewell, gentlemen soldiers. Is the young witcher in the saddle yet?"

"Already," Geralt answered.

"Then on the road. After me."

The Upper March set the mark of its border above the Gwenllech. It is well known that the ambition of the local margraves is to make further incursions into the valleys of the Dragon Mountains, and so they are wont to call their domain cis-montane, as if it were soon to be enlarged by a transmontane part, once the elves have been driven deeper into the mountains. Yet the years pass, and nothing of the sort comes to be.

Baldwin Adovardo, Regni Caedvenie Nova Descriptio




Chapter Two


The kingdom of Kaedwen was known throughout the inhabited world for weather cold and capricious. Shut off to the north by the barrier of the Dragon Mountains, and to the east by the mighty massif of the Blue Mountains, the land suffered from the frequent and unpredictable wanderings of air masses, which brought long and bitter winters, cold springs, and short, rainy summers. As for autumn, that varied: sometimes it was sunny, warm, and pleasant, and sometimes not at all.

Now, in the month of March, called Birke by the elves, snow still lay here and there in gullies and uprooted hollows, in white patches in the sinkings out on the barrens. Ice, in yellowing sheets, still covered some puddles and ditches. The little sun seemed to warm a bit, but when the wind came off the mountains, it burned with frost no less than in January.

Geralt set out from Kaer Morhen the day before the Equinox. Such was the witchers' custom. It had been learned by practice that after winter monsters were at their hungriest, and so aggressive that folk in villages and settlements were ready to shell out for a witcher's hire, though in the hungry gap they had eaten through their stores and were practically without means to live. But Geralt had no chance to get hired. It turned out as it turned out, barely two days' ride from the mountains, bang, a peasant and his daughter, marauders, a bald bruiser with bad teeth, hack, slash, there you are. He landed before the court of Headman Bulava in the village of Neuhold, and from that court, and then from the threat of lynching at the hands of soldiers from the nearby garrison, he was rescued by that strange white-haired personage with two swords on his back and a black horse under him, whose trail Geralt was now following.

"I propose," said the strange personage, turning in the saddle, "that we ride together for a while. Captain Carleton may still want to hang you; one could see he wanted it very much. He is not quite so stupid as to chase me, but you, alone, may be an easy mark. So if my company does not inconvenience you..."

"Not at all," Geralt assured him hurriedly, urging on his dun mare. "Gladly... I... I'm..."

"I know who you are. Did the mutations whiten your hair? The Changes? A loss of pigment, as with me?"

"Aye... But how..."

"How do I know what you are? Because I keep track of what goes on up there in your Keep. And word reached me that a certain wunderkind had trained there, one called Geralt, and that before long he was to set out on the Path."




"But Vesemir...

“He never mentioned me? The name Preston Holt never slipped out? Let me explain: Vesemir and I have for some time been moving in different orbits, so to speak. If you know what I mean.”

Geralt did not quite know what an orbit was, but he nodded with a wise expression.

For a while they rode in silence. Side by side.

“So then you set out from Kaer Morhen upon the Path,” Preston Holt said at last. “Your beginning may not have been the best, but such are beginnings. I, for my part, have no thought of reproaching you; quite the contrary, I looked over the corpse of that marauder, and your cuts must be judged impeccable. Perhaps unnecessary, perhaps ill-considered, perhaps inelegant, but all in all impeccable.”

They fell silent again, watching a herd of cattle driven out onto the high pasture, and a little shepherd boy running from cow to cow to warm his frozen little feet in a nice fresh warm cowpat. The cowpat probably did only a middling job of warming them, but the running certainly did.

“They’re driving the cows out,” Holt observed, “though the grass is barely poking out of the ground. Which means the season has begun and you will find work without difficulty, Geralt. The villages will soon be willing to pay for protection for their herdsmen and livestock. Let us urge on our horses, there, into the birch copse, along the edge of the tailrace.”

“The edge of what?”

“The tailrace. This ditch here is a tailrace, a channel by which water was once drained from the mine. We are, as you doubtless know, in that part of the kingdom of Kaedwen known as the Upper March. The wealth of the Upper March lies in minerals: chiefly salt, but also silver, nickel, zinc, lead, lapis lazuli and the like. So it was, at any rate, once; nowadays most of those minerals seem to have begun to run short. Nothing lasts forever.”

Geralt made no comment.

“Do you see that hill ahead of us? It is called Podkurek; that is how it appears on the official maps. And the name came from this: a hundred years ago or so, a peasant by the name of Podkurek happened to dig up there a lump of silver the size of a fair big cabbage head. A mine sprang up here at once and bit into the mountainside. Great quantities of silver and galena, lead ore, were extracted there. But the deeper the miners dug, the greater trouble they had with water. There are more tailraces like this one; you will see them. At last the cost of drainage rendered the whole undertaking unprofitable. The miners moved away elsewhere. They left behind a labyrinth of galleries and workings, partly flooded. And now the best part: the abandoned and flooded mine was occupied and taken under the rule of wandering ratlings. You do know what ratlings are, I trust?”

“Ratlings,” Geralt recited, drawing breath, “are small creatures resembling dog-headed apes. Pack-dwelling. They live underground, in darkness. In a pack they are dangerous...”

“Dangerous as hell,” Preston Holt cut the recitation short. “And they quite often make life miserable for amateur miners, who come scratching about in the slopes of Podkurek in search of the silver one can still dig up here. Oh, and there is an illustration of my remarks: what is gleaming over there is wagon cloth and tents. We shall ride straight into the camp of those valiant diggers. The first this spring.”

The valiant diggers greeted them first with a delegation armed with shovels and clubs. The threatening looks on the greeters’ faces sent a simple message: off with you, strangers, we were here first. But the looks soon softened, for the newcomers were not, as inspection showed, unwanted rivals. And on faces which not long before had still been hostile, sheer joy painted itself.

“Olaboga, thanks be to the gods!” cried the chief digger, hiding behind his back the mattock he had only a moment before been brandishing by way of warning. “Thanks be to the gods! Olaboga, why, it’s the noble lord witcher himself! And seeing as word reached us you was in these parts, we’d already been meaning to send for you! And meanwhile you’ve come to us like from heaven, like from heaven!”

“It is a habit of mine.” Preston Holt straightened in the saddle. “To fall from heaven upon those in need. For I am a witcher.”

Meanwhile several wailing dames had joined the diggers. It was getting louder and more incoherent. With gesture and voice Preston Holt set about demanding order. It took a while before it could finally be made out what the matter was and what it was the diggers and their women really wanted.

“Witcher needed, witcher!” cried the chief digger, waving his mattock. “Them underground creatures what live under the mountain carried off our lad! Set on him, snatched him, dragged him into the chasm! Who’s to save him now, if not you?”

“It cannot be even a fortnight,” said Holt, “since I warned you to keep away from the shaft and adit. Well? Did I not? To dig on the other side of the mountain? You say zatrawce carried the boy off? How old? Five? Long ago? Ha, the day before yesterday? You were in no hurry to send for me. Dismount, Geralt.”

Preston Holt dismounted. Geralt could not help noticing that it was not easy for him. And that he limped badly on his left leg. With a gesture he drove off the womenfolk crowding round him and wailing. He spoke for a moment with the elder digger, then tugged Geralt by the sleeve.

“Well then, young witcher,” he said. “We shall have to help folk in need, folk wronged by monsters. For we have just been hired.”

“If the zatrawce carried the boy off the day before yesterday,” Geralt muttered, “there’s precious little chance he’s still...”

“Alive? Yes, that is doubtful. But perhaps at least we shall find... Hmm... Give the mother a chance to bury what remains... Why the hesitation, young Geralt? Only a moment ago you fling yourself without a thought to defend a maiden’s honour and slash a man to death, and now hesitation?”

“Did I say anything?” Geralt shrugged. “Or that I’m hesitating? I’m not hesitating.”

On the mountainside remnants of wooden structures had survived, no doubt horse whims. A shaft gaped there, partly caved in, baring a chasm.

"What remains of the mine," Holt explained, "is the shaft, and the adit too, there, farther on. Both lead to the upper working. The working twists, for it runs along the vein, now completely exhausted. From there a decline runs down, leading to the lower working, more twisting still, with numerous branches. Below that is yet another adit, but wholly flooded. The ratlings lair in that lower gallery. There may also be some holes there, connections to natural caves."

"The plan, then, is this: with my leg I shall not be going down, so I shall make for the adit and raise a din there, draw the ratlings to me. Then all hope lies in you. You will go down the shaft to the gallery, then take the decline down. There you may have a chance of finding... what must be found. With a little luck. Which I wish you, my boy. We shall meet again above."

"Maybe first," Geralt ventured, "check out..."

"Do what?" Holt grimaced. "Ah, I understand. There is no point, however, in... checking anything out. Drink your elixir, get your medallion in hand, and climb down into the shaft."

"With all due respect," the chief digger standing beside them blurted, "but that youngster there looks awful young somehow... Will he manage? We thought it'd be you, Master Holt, going down. Your very own self..."

Preston Holt turned and looked at him. The digger shrank, mumbled. And did not finish.





The gallery was high; Geralt could walk fully upright and there were still a few handspans to spare before the roof. Water dripped everywhere from the walls, and apart from the sound of the falling drops nothing could be heard. He quickened his pace, wanting to reach the decline as soon as possible, before Holt made good on his promise and kicked up a racket at the adit.

Deep caverns gaped in the walls of the working, traces of gouged-out pockets of ore. In one such cavity he noticed a strange brick structure, or rather the remains of one. He could not have known that these were the remnants of an ancient shrine.

Geralt did not know it either, but miners believed in gods longer than any other social group. Working under conditions of constant danger, they had to believe that some providence watched over them, and that prayers offered up to it would keep them safe. As one might easily guess, practice soon proved that prayers were good for nothing, that cave-ins and firedamp explosions happened just as often to the pious as to unbelievers. But the miners believed all the same, built shrines, lit candles and prayed. For a long time. But not forever. Reason, as usual, won in the end.

The passage suddenly began to slope down; he was on the decline now. He cocked an ear, but still heard nothing save dripping water.

He seemed to be almost at the lower gallery when it began.

Out of the darkness a large stone whistled past, grazing his hair. Others flew after it, and some hit. One struck Geralt on the head and staggered him, and the ratlings came at him from all sides, howling and barking wildly, clawing and biting. There was no counting them; they were too quick, swarming around him. Geralt yanked his sword from the scabbard only to lose it at once: two creatures pinned him down, a third knocked the weapon from his hand with a blow from a huge rock. The creature raised the captured sword aloft and roared in triumph, opening its doglike maw to its full width. The triumph was premature: Geralt flung off both attackers, snatched up a stone and hurled it, hitting the ratling straight in the teeth. The ratling dropped the sword, Geralt leapt, caught the weapon before it fell, and cut down the creature wrestling with the stone jammed in its mouth. With two quick cuts he split open two more. And flung himself toward the shaft. Behind him came wild barking and flying stones, again with some finding their mark. One struck him in the back of the head: a flash, a blaze of stars lit up the working. The second hit him across the small of the back and nearly knocked him down. Twice the ratlings caught him, sinking their teeth into his calves; his leather greaves spared him serious injury. One leapt in and bit him painfully above the knee, where the greave gave no protection. Geralt did not slow to kill it.

Under a hail of stones he reached the shaft, climbed the remains of the ladder, surfaced, fell there and lay. For a while.

“Well, well,” he heard. “You got out. And without any serious injuries either. Blood, I see, is running from a few places. But nowhere is it pouring, and that is something already. I am full of admiration.”

Preston Holt stood over him, gnawing at the leg of a roast chicken.

“Oh, come on,” Geralt groaned, still lying there. “Oh, come on! You were supposed to be in the adit... Make noise... Draw them off...”

“Indeed?” Holt threw away the bone. “Ah, yes. Forgive me. I quite forgot.”

Geralt swore. His mouth still against the ground.

“What’s more,” Holt said, licking his fingers, “they’ll pay us nothing. The boy turned up. Simply wandered in. He’d merely been roaming somewhere, and the diggers, as usual, laid the blame on the ratlings. Up, young Geralt. Witcher Geralt. Let me help you. Can you walk? Then let us go. As I said, no dice on the pay. But they’ll feed us and put us up for the night. The girls will dress your cuts. If you ask nicely, perhaps one of them will be nice to you.”

They went toward the encampment and the steaming cauldrons. Witcher Geralt could barely put one foot in front of the other.

Chubby-cheeked girls dressed him in a few places. And fed him. And the diggers let them stay the night. Holt in a tent, Geralt on a wagon.

One of the girls came to Geralt in the night and was nice to him. But only nice, only a little, and nothing more. Then she went away at once.

At dawn Geralt hauled himself down from the wagon and began saddling the mare, still hissing with pain. Holt caught him at it.

"Why the hurry?" He rubbed his eye. "Wait. They’ll treat us to breakfast, then we’ll ride on."

"Oh, come on," Geralt ground out. "What if I’m not at all sure I want to go anywhere with you? What if I’d rather ride on alone?"

Holt leaned against a birch trunk and studied the sky. Clear, without a single cloud.

"I fully understand," he said, "your attitude. But I had to, and I stress had to, put you through a preliminary test, to see what you’re like in action."

"I might not have come out of there."

"But you did."

"Not thanks to your help. So now..."

"I ask you," Holt interrupted, "to ride with me at least until noon. Which, reckoning in round numbers and allowing for the hour of day, comes to five or six stages. That time and that distance will suffice, I think, for you to work off the anger you bear me and look at the world with a clearer head. And by then I shall have a proposal for you."

"What proposal?" Geralt narrowed his eyes.

"Six stages. At noon."

It was in fact close to noon when the sky suddenly blackened with wings and rang with the beating of feathers and one great cawing. Dozens, if not hundreds, of black birds rose from the ground and from the branches of the surrounding trees and flew off.

"Ravens," Geralt sighed. "So many ravens! It can’t be! Ravens don’t fly in flocks! Never!"

"Beyond doubt," Holt conceded. "So many ravens at once is an unheard-of thing; I myself am amazed. Beyond doubt we are dealing with an uncommon phenomenon. And we also find ourselves in a rather uncommon place. If you noticed."

"A crossroads." Geralt looked around. "Where roads part."

"A crossroads. A symbolic place. Four roads, to the four corners of the world. A place of choice and decision. A choice you must now make, Geralt. Witcher Geralt."

The ravens settled on the higher branches of the trees. They cawed, watching the riders.

"Three of the roads, including ours, are the roads of your solitary witcher’s life, the fate you chose when you set out on the Path from Kaer Morhen. If you take one of those three roads, if that is your decision, we shall part. If, however, you choose the fourth road, you will hear my proposal."

The ravens cawed.

"I am, as you have no doubt noticed, advanced in years. I will add, on my own account, that I am very advanced in years. You would be astonished how very. I am also, and this too is plain to see and impossible to hide, a cripple. The days of my witcher glory are already a song of the past. I shall no longer take to the Path with swords; the brightness of my blades, if I may put it so, will no longer drive back the dark. But the dark exists, monsters still go pounding about by night. You can face them and defeat them. People endangered by monsters await your help."

"Quite right, here I am waxing poetic while you're waiting for a proposal. Here it is: I propose we work together. I was well known here in Kaedwen, and still am; I never lacked for clients, and even now people ask me for help I can no longer give them. But you can. I've had a look at you, and I say: be my successor, Geralt. Instead of wandering the highways hungry, come and live with me. Make use of my renown, and you will not lack for work. And after work you'll have somewhere to come back to. And somewhere to winter. As for me... It will make me happy that someone is continuing my work. And also, I do not hide it, that someone will help support me in my old age."

"You needn't decide at once. For now, it is enough if you go on not alone, but with me. Bear me company a while yet. Agreed?"

"Well, I don't know... Agreed."

"To the road, then. Let us leave this crossroads to the ravens."

The ravens saw them off with their cawing.

"Do not take offence," Holt said, turning in the saddle, "but I insist that, in my presence, you express yourself more or less properly. In particular, that you not say 'check it out' and 'come on.'"





The Western March lies on the Buina, and to the west rests against the Pustula Mountains. It had been the earlier intent of the margraves to push the border farther and farther westward, but naught came of it, for there the kingdom of Hengfors marked out its eastern frontier. There was dispute over this, and bloodshed threatened. Hengfors, though small, had a mighty protector in Kovir, and so the margraves had to swallow their pride and agree to peace.

The peace, concluded in the year 1225 post Resurectionem and called the Peace of Hołopole, established the boundary between the two kingdoms on the Braa, and thereby fixed the western verge of the March as its non plus ultra.

Baldwin Adovardo, Regni Caedvenie Nova Descriptio





Chapter Three


As usual, cats and children were the first to notice them. The cats, of which there were any number on the edge of the little town, yielded the road unwillingly; when they moved off, they turned their heads and hissed. The children fled into the houses with crying and howls, abandoning what they had been playing with, mainly lumps of dried mud.

Apart from the cats and children, none of the inhabitants of the little town of Spynham paid the incoming witchers the slightest attention. You might have thought riders in black cloaks with swords on their backs came into Spynham so regularly that they had ceased to interest anyone at all.

Preston Holt knew the place, knew which stable to leave the horses in. Then they went on foot, down a muddy alley, scattering more cats and more snotty brats.

"Great lord," a beggar woman squatting by the wall with an infant on her lap called out to Geralt. "Great lord, a penny... For milk for the baby..."





Before Holt could react, Geralt fished a mark from his purse and tossed it to the beggar woman, who thanked him profusely. Holt kept silent for some fifty paces, then at last stopped.

"You're no great lord," he ground out, looking Geralt in the eye. "What you are is a great fool. A mark will buy the yield from twelve cows. And you, as you'll soon find out, they'll want to pay only a little more than that for killing a monster. And killing a monster takes a bit more effort than snivelling for alms and baring rotten teeth. And the baby was probably borrowed from some crony who's sitting in a tavern waiting for a customer so she can suck him off in the privy for half a mark. Fair warning: don't come down this alley again. There'll be a big laugh if you show up here once more."

For a moment Geralt wanted to stand up to Holt and talk back to him, tell him to leave him alone and mind his own business. He didn't. Somehow, by reflex, he had taken Holt for someone senior to him, almost a master. Perhaps because Holt reminded him strongly of Vesemir. In manner and speech. Physically he was very different.

Ahead of them gleamed the temple's whitewashed colonnades, and beneath them squatted a whole army of beggars, women and men. The temple had stood shut and abandoned for years, but the beggars still squatted there. And held out their hands for alms. From habit.

Prudently, Holt drew Geralt over to the other side of the street.

"Do you know why we're called witchers?" he asked. "Because we are the children of witches."

"Oh, come off it..." Geralt faltered. "I mean, what a load of rubbish. Witchers from witches. Give it a rest."



"It may strike you as amusing, but this happens to be true. The first witchers were the children of women with uncontrolled magical abilities, called witches. They were not entirely sound of mind, and lusty young men often used them as sexual playthings. The children, the fruits of such amusements, were abandoned. Or left on doorsteps. And from orphanages and foundling homes they sometimes made their way to witcher schools."

"Yeah, right. You made that up. It wasn't like that."

"It was. All us witchers are descended from feeble-minded girls. Doesn't that amuse you?"

"Not a bit. Wasn't like that at all."

"It was, it was. But a long time ago! These days you won't find witches beneath temples. The sorcerers exterminated them all. Ha! Nothing lasts forever."

The closer they came to the market square, the more crowded it grew. For Geralt it was something new. He was not used to crowds and felt ill at ease in them. The noise irritated him. He was starting to feel short of breath, and the stench was becoming more and more oppressive. It reeked of smoke, scorched fat, rotting fruit, dung, and the devil knew what else.

On the square they had to push into a row of stalls and a throng of buyers. Here too nothing awaited Geralt but novelties and surprises. And, finally, wonder. Never in his life had he imagined there were so many crafts in the world, or that such an abundance of wares was offered for sale. And that so many were eager to buy. Straps and leather goods, glazed clay pots and unglazed, fur caps, short sheepskin coats, hide shoes, embroidered kerchiefs, copper frying pans, rakes, pitchforks, hoe-hafts - and pretzels, pretzels, pretzels.

Holt paid not the slightest heed to the marvels on the stalls. But at one point he stopped shoving his way through the crowd and caught Geralt by the sleeve.

"Eyes down," he hissed. "Eyes down, don't stare."

"Why?"

"Sorceresses."

Geralt obediently averted his gaze. Reluctantly. The two women by the stall with amber jewellery drew the eye with truly magnetic force. With their rich clothes. And their picture-book beauty.

"They," Holt explained when they had moved away, "take ordinary people for cattle, and they hate witchers. They're quick to quarrel, and they may take staring for a provocation. Better, too, that they don't sense our medallions."

Holt stopped before an imposing house, above the door of which a signboard had been nailed up. The painting on the sign, quite well done, depicted a mermaid with an ample bosom. The words beneath the mermaid read: 'Lorelei.'

Holt seized the knocker and knocked energetically.

Very energetically.

The massive door opened, and a broad-shouldered bruiser stood in it, with a jaw like a loaf of bread. For a long moment the bruiser eyed the witchers. Then he stepped back and moved aside in invitation.

Holt, plainly a habitué, took both swords from his back without being asked and handed them to the bruiser. Geralt hastened to follow his example.

Disarmed, they entered an antechamber lit by several lamps. Geralt had trouble catching his breath; the antechamber was thick with the scent of perfume and incense. On the wall hung another painted mermaid. Just as busty. There was no inscription.

"Pan Preston Holt," said the woman as she entered.

"Pani Pampinea Monteforte." Holt bowed. Geralt bowed too. And shut the mouth he had just opened.

In admiration.

Untutored in the arts of ladies' dressmaking, Geralt could not have known that the materials of Pampinea Monteforte's black gown were transparent chiffon, muslin and crape. Nor did he know that it was a great art to sew a gown so that it covered even as it revealed.

And vice versa.

Nor did Geralt know what the quintessence of femininity was.

But what did it matter that he did not know, when he saw.
